title: Stop Asking ChatGPT To Compute RSI. It’s Embarrassing For Both Of You.
author: Outrageous_Fall_1727
selftext:
*Why technical analysis belongs in a tool, not a context window.* A 4 minute read on why you shouldn't paste raw market data (or any data to be used for computing) to your AI assisntant.

One thing that feels broken in a lot of AI workflows is that we keep using language models for the one part of the job that should be deterministic. If I ask a model what RSI means, what divergences matter, or how to interpret a bullish crossover in context, great. If I ask it to compute a 14-period indicator from a long table of prices while preserving every smoothing detail, that’s where the whole thing starts to feel like a very expensive magic trick.

I’m curious how other people draw that boundary between “reasoning” and “computation” in agent workflows. My view is that indicator calculation belongs in tools, and the model should stick to summarising, comparing, and explaining. I wrote up that argument in a short article after getting increasingly annoyed by this exact pattern.

TLDR of the article: don't ask Claude to do complex math.

comments.author: TrustedEssentials
comments.body: Language models are architects, not calculators. I run a fully automated algorithmic trading bot, and the boundary between reasoning and computation is absolute. I use AI strictly to write the Python logic, build the system architecture, and troubleshoot the API connections. When the market is open, the script runs on standard math libraries pulling live institutional data. The AI never touches the actual equations.

replies.author: ConstructionOld5043
replies.body: yup I do the same. here is my work. bullbearxinsights.app
